text: Robotic V-Pulse Procedure on a Da Vinci X System Presented by Dr. Somshekhar SP, Head of the Department and Consultant Surgical Oncologist, Manipal Comprehensive Cancer Centre, Bangalore

A male patient with a ECOG performance status of zero presented to us with complaints of yellowish discoloration of sclera, off and on since three months, passage of pale-colored stool since one month, itching all over the body since one month. Patient had consulted outside and ERCP biopsy with CBD stenting was done. There were no other significant complaints or comorbidities.
Ultrasound revealed the dilated CBD and GB distended with IHBRD. CT scan showed a mass lesion in the peri-ampullary region with dilated CBD of and no evidence of ascites or metastasis.

Biopsy from the peri-ampullary region was a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with CA-NT-Nine of two point three. Current bilirubin was three point two with a direct of two point seven and albumin three point five.
Hence, patient was worked up and robotic whipples planned.
The patient is positioned supine with legs separated, allowing the surgical assistant to perform manipulations through the assistant port during the robotic pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a Veress needle technique to insufflate the abdomen, preparing the surgical field for the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.

The surgical team demonstrates the port placement technique for the robotic pancreaticoduodenectomy. The camera arm is positioned approximately two finger-breadths below the umbilicus. Robotic ports R-I and R-III are placed near the splenic and hepatic flexures, respectively, while R-II is positioned one finger-breadth above the midpoint between the camera and R-III. Two assistant ports are triangulated between the camera and R-II, and the camera and R-I.
The robotic surgical system is docked at the head end of the patient, who is positioned in a fifteen-degree reverse Trendelenburg position.
The following picture represents our plan of resection as well as reconstruction in this case.

The diagram shows classical six steps of a V-pulse pancreatic odiodenectomy, which includes exposure of a infrapancreatic SMV, extended caucarization, portal dissection, stomach transduction, jejunal transduction, and transduction of pancreas.
The orientation of the robotic instruments is shown for approaching the target anatomy in the following diagram.
The robotic instrument R-I is equipped with monopolar curved scissors, R-II with a vessel sealer, and R-III with a fenestrated bipolar forceps, ready for use in the surgical procedure.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac to expose the pancreas, taking care to avoid injury to the gastroepiploic arcade, which is visible near the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches cranially towards the splenic region to mobilize the stomach, revealing the pancreas as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and creates a tunnel between the SMV and the neck of the pancreas. The middle colic vein is visible opening into the SMV. The absence of tributaries on the anterior aspect of the SMV facilitates tunnel formation. Near the cranial end, the junction between the portal vein and the SMV, known as the SMV-PV junction, is visible.
Following the completion of the tunnel formation, the surgeon begins to open the lesser omentum, taking care to avoid the accessory left hepatic artery, which may be present.

The surgeon completes circumferential mobilization of the stomach and transects it using a stapler introduced through the assistant port. The transection level is determined based on the non-pylorus preserving approach of the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon begins dissection along the superior border of the pancreas, revealing the common hepatic artery and a constant lymph node located in this region.
The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of the constant node located near the common hepatic artery along the superior border of the pancreas.

The surgeon performs portal dissection, carefully dissecting the hepatoduodenal ligament to expose the common hepatic duct, common bile duct, and cystic duct. A lymph node in this region is excised. The right hepatic artery is preserved during dissection. Moving caudally, the gastro-duodenal artery arising from the common hepatic artery is visualized, and the supra-duodenal artery branch is ligated.
The surgeon clips the gastro-duodenal artery, ensuring an adequate length is maintained for proper hemostasis.
The surgeon initiates extended caucarization, mobilizing the duodenum and exposing the inferior vena cava up to its junction with the left renal vein.

The surgeon begins the transaction of the pancreas using a harmonic scalpel introduced through port RII for parenchymal dissection. A monopolar hot shears is used to sharply cut the pancreatic duct. The uncinate process of the pancreas is approached and dissected from the lateral border of the superior mesenteric vein using the harmonic scalpel. The gallbladder is further dissected from the liver bed.
The video shows the cystic duct joining the common hepatic duct, with an accessory bile duct visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon encircles the common hepatic duct with umbilical tape and transects it just above the junction with the cystic duct and common bile duct. The previously placed stent is removed for culture sensitivity testing. The transverse mesocolon is opened, revealing underlying structures.

The surgeon initiates mobilization of the duodenal jejunal flexure region, isolating the jejunum for transaction. A surgical stapler is introduced through the assistant port to perform the jejunal transaction.

Following the jejunal transaction, the surgeon lateralizes the specimen and initiates dissection of the uncinate process of the pancreas. Care is taken to avoid the branches of the inferior pancreaticoduodenal vessels, which are visible and could lead to postoperative hemorrhage. The specimen is now completely isolated.

The surgeon brings the distal jejunal loop through the opening in the transverse mesocolon, ensuring there is no undue traction or tension. A ductomucosa pancreatic jejunostomy is performed to restore continuity between the remnant pancreas and the jejunum. The anterior and posterior parts are anastomosed using 4-0 Prolene sutures in a continuous fashion. An opening is made in the jejunum with the tip of monopolar hot shears, and the continuity between the pancreatic duct and jejunal mucosa is restored using interrupted 4-0 or 5-0 monofilament absorbable sutures.

The surgeon begins by targeting the four quadrants at the six o'clock position, followed by twelve o'clock, three o'clock, and nine o'clock positions. An internal stent is introduced into the surgical site.
The surgeon begins the closure of the anterior layer using a continuous suture technique with proline.
The surgeon performs a hepaticojejunostomy, suturing the posterior layer in a continuous manner. A jejunostomy is created using monopolar hot shear, ensuring the anastomosis is watertight.
The surgeon takes continuous rows of sutures to cover the anterior layer, using a tagging suture to suspend the jejunum from the undersurface of the liver.
The surgeon performs the gastrojejunostomy by burying the previously stapled suture line and placing a tagging suture at the anastomosis site between the stomach and the jejunum.
The surgeon creates a posterior fourth layer, followed by performing a jejunostomy and a gastrostomy. A stapled gastrojejunostomy is completed, and the resulting defect is closed.
The final view after reconstruction shows the completed gastrojejunostomy and feeding jejunostomy, with all anastomoses appearing intact and well-positioned.

The surgeon presents the complete specimen retrieved through a small incision in the supraumbilical region, showing a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the ampullary region. The specimen's margins are free of tumor, with three out of nineteen lymph nodes involved.
